summary: Spolsky argues that winning customers from an established competitor requires systematically eliminating every barrier to switching, not just the obvious ones. Using Microsoft Excel's conquest of Lotus 123 as the central case study, he shows how each barrier removed roughly doubles potential conversions. The critical non-obvious insight is that the fear of switching *back* is often the biggest barrier to switching *in* — people won't try your product if they feel trapped. Excel's tipping point came not when it could read Lotus files, but when it could write them, letting users participate in the Lotus ecosystem while using Excel. Spolsky argues that making it easy for customers to leave your product is counterintuitively the best way to get them to arrive. Trying to lock in customers before they've even switched to you only locks them out.
key_points: Barriers to switching are cumulative — with 8-9 barriers, virtually no one will overcome all of them, so eliminating even one barrier can double your conversion rate.
The most overlooked barrier isn't the difficulty of switching in, but the fear of how hard it will be to switch back out if the new product doesn't work.
Excel's tipping point came with version 4.0, which could write Lotus 123 files — not just read them — allowing users to participate in the existing ecosystem.
Attempting to create lock-in before you've won the customer is counterproductive; it becomes lock-out instead.
Companies in competitive markets must honestly acknowledge they need people to switch from a dominant competitor rather than pretending the competitor doesn't matter.
money_quotes: The only strategy in getting people to switch to your product is to eliminate barriers.
Eliminating just one barrier will likely double your sales.
That's the barrier to entry. Not how hard it is to switch in: it's how hard it might be to switch out.
Try to lock them in before they become your customers, and you'll just lock them out.
Nobody wants to switch to a product that is going to eliminate their freedom in the future.
The mature approach to strategy is not to try to force things on potential customers.
Most managers only think about strategy one step at a time, like chess players who refuse to think one move ahead.
Shakespeare and Company on Manhattan's Upper West Side did not close because Barnes and Nobles had cheaper prices, it closed because Barnes and Nobles had more human beings in the building.
key_insight: The biggest barrier preventing customers from switching to your product isn't the cost of switching in — it's the fear of how hard it will be to switch back out if things don't work.
